title: C0 Stability of Topological Entropy for 3-Dimensional Reeb Flows
speaker: Matthias Meiwes
abstract: The C0 distance on the space of contact forms on a contact manifold has been studied recently by different authors. It can be thought of as an analogue for Reeb flows of the Hofer metric on the space of Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms. In this talk, I will explain some recent progress on the stability properties of the topological entropy with respect to this distance obtained in collaboration with M. Alves, L. Dahinden, and A. Pirnapasov. Our main result states that the topological entropy for closed contact 3-manifolds is lower semi-continuous in the C0 distance for C∞-generic contact froms.

proof_stages.label: C0 distance, entropy, and main stability problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces contact forms and Reeb flows, the C0/contact Banach-Mazur distance and its metric analogue, topological entropy, and states the main lower-semicontinuity theorem together with metric and right-handed-flow corollaries and the broader conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Hypertight links and homotopical entropy bounds
proof_stages.summary: Hypertightness in the complement of a Reeb link, proper link classes, and cylindrical contact homology growth are set up so that homotopical growth of proper classes gives a lower bound for topological entropy.

proof_stages.label: Approximating entropy by unique-orbit link classes
proof_stages.summary: Positive entropy is converted into links whose proper homotopy classes carrying exactly one periodic orbit have exponential growth arbitrarily close to the entropy, via three-dimensional flow versions of Katok-type symbolic dynamics and fitted surfaces.

proof_stages.label: C0 stability theorem for homotopical growth
proof_stages.summary: The key stability theorem says that if a nondegenerate form is hypertight relative to a link with positive unique-orbit class growth, then every sufficiently C0-close contact form has some link whose proper classes retain nearly the same exponential growth, even though the new link need not be isotopic or hypertight.

proof_stages.label: Deduction of lower semicontinuity
proof_stages.summary: The approximation theorem, addition of global-surface-of-section bindings, the stability theorem, the entropy lower bound, and Newhouse upper semicontinuity are combined to prove lower semicontinuity on a C2-open and C∞-dense set, with a stronger conclusion for nondegenerate surface metrics.

proof_stages.label: Cobordism stretching and persistence of the link
proof_stages.summary: To prove the stability theorem, long exact symplectic cobordisms are built and parametrized moduli spaces of finite-energy cylinders are used to carry the original link to disjoint holomorphic cylinders, with action bounds and positivity of intersections giving compactness and an ambient isotopy.

proof_stages.label: Compactness for curves in proper link classes
proof_stages.summary: For curves representing proper link classes, where action estimates no longer control large periods, the cylinder isotopy and hypertightness in the link complement rule out bubbling and breaking such as finite-energy planes avoiding the link.

proof_stages.label: Limiting buildings and retained growth
proof_stages.summary: Letting the neck length tend to infinity produces holomorphic buildings whose asymptotic orbits define the new link for the perturbed form, and asymptotic analysis controls possible collapse of components so that exponential class growth survives.

examples.name: Bumped two-torus geodesic flow
examples.note: A Riemannian two-torus with a large bump has lifted geodesics choosing to pass above or below each bump, giving exponentially many separated trajectories and making positive entropy visually concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws out the bumped torus in the universal cover, explains choosing above/below each bump to produce many geodesics, and later relates these lifted geodesics to separated sets and positive entropy.

examples.name: Small C0 elevation creating short closed geodesics
examples.note: Raising a flat part of a surface and rounding corners can be C0-small while creating arbitrarily short closed geodesics, illustrating the flexibility of the C0 metric on metrics/contact forms.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes a concrete C0-small elevation of a flat region, with rounded corners, and uses it to create closed geodesics of arbitrarily small length.

passes.reason: The perturbed metric is structurally ordinary but is used to show that small C0 distance does not prevent arbitrarily short closed geodesics.
A smooth small C0 perturbation with tiny closed geodesics is used to show C0 closeness does not control short geodesics.
The arbitrarily short closed geodesics form a limiting small-length construction used to show C0 closeness does not control such geodesics.
A small ordinary metric elevation is used as an enemy example showing C0-closeness can still create arbitrarily short closed geodesics.
The small C0 elevation is used as an adversarial illustration that C0 closeness does not control the creation of very short closed geodesics.
The C0-small elevation drives closed-geodesic lengths arbitrarily toward zero and is used to show naive C0 control of the length/dynamics fails.
The small C0 elevation is used to show that C0 closeness does not control the creation of arbitrarily short closed geodesics.
the small C0 perturbation drives closed-geodesic lengths arbitrarily close to zero, showing C0 closeness does not control that length scale
The C0-small elevation approaches the zero-systole boundary and is used to show C0 closeness need not control short closed geodesics.
a small C0 perturbation with arbitrarily short closed geodesics is used to show C0 closeness does not control the length spectrum.
The tiny C0 perturbation is an adversarial example showing C0 closeness does not control short closed geodesics, and it realizes an arbitrarily small-length limiting feature.
the C0-small elevation is an adversarial illustration that C0 closeness can still create closed geodesics with length arbitrarily near zero

examples.name: Right-handed flows from pinched geodesic flows
examples.note: Pinched metrics whose geodesic flows lift to right-handed flows on S^3 provide a concrete family to which the lower-semicontinuity corollary applies.
examples.engagement_reason: Pinched geodesic flows lifting to right-handed flows on S3 are cited as a known source of examples and a corollary setting, without checking right-handedness or working through a particular pinched metric.

examples.name: Hamiltonian surface-diffeomorphism comparison
examples.note: In Q&A the speaker contrasts the Reeb-flow proof with the analogous Hofer-metric result for Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms of surfaces, where Floer continuation avoids the bubbling and link-isotopy difficulties present here.
examples.engagement_reason: Hamiltonian surface diffeomorphisms are used as a comparison point for prior Hofer-metric continuity and proof strategy, but no particular diffeomorphism or calculation is worked out.
